query: evidence on perampanel use in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pseudobulbar affect is commonly seen in PLS, and recognition with screening and early treatment with medications can reduce social withdrawal and positively impact patients' quality of life. Treatment with dextromethorphan/quinidine is commonly used. In patients who cannot tolerate dextromethorphan/quinidine or do not benefit from treatment, amitriptyline or fluvoxamine can be trialed. However, neither has been studied in controlled trials for the pseudobulbar effect. [1] Screening for mood symptoms, especially depression, is essential, and early referral to a psychiatrist or therapist is recommended when appropriate. Riluzole, a Food and Drug Administration-approved drug for ALS that provides a modest survival benefit of a few months, has not shown survival benefits in patients with PLS and is currently not recommended in the treatment of PLS. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Dentatorubral Pallidoluysian Atrophy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: When there is clinical suspicion of seizures; selecting an anti-epileptic drug (AED) could be individualized to the predominant clinical scenario of the seizures. No AED is found superior to others in randomized controlled studies. However, levetiracetam and perampanel show promise in juvenile-onset DRPLA. [38] [46] [47] Choreoathetoid and dystonic movements are treated with tetrabenazine, risperidone, and gabapentin. [42] [48] Consensus European guidelines show that riluzole (Class B evidence) or amantadine (Class C evidence) can potentially improve ataxic symptoms. [49] [50] [51] [52] Given unknown teratogenicity, however, they are avoided in childbearing patients or currently pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Riluzole, Edaravone, and sodium phenylbutyrate are the only approved medications to treat ALS but provide limited survival benefits. [1] [3] Riluzole, 50mg bid, is recommended for all patients with ALS and is thought to reduce glutamate-induced excitotoxicity. Riluzole is the only medication that improves overall survival (74% vs. 58% in the placebo group at 12 months) and slows the progression of symptoms (ie, survival without tracheostomy (57% vs. 50% at 18 months). Riluzole is most effective in patients who have been symptomatic for less than 5 years, have a vital capacity (VC) >60% of the predicted value, and do not require a tracheostomy. [36] It is important to note that the elimination of riluzole will be affected by CYP1A2 inhibitors like caffeine and theophylline (decrease the rate of elimination of riluzole). Transaminase elevation occurs in approximately 50% of patients. Monitor transaminases monthly for the first three months and every three months after.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: AVP-923 (dextromethorphan-quinidine 20mg/10mg) has proven effective in patients with pseudobulbar affect. [45] Less rigorous evidence has supported the use of amitriptyline and fluvoxamine in smaller trials. Pain is treated based on its underlying cause. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications, paracetamol, intra-articular steroids, or lidocaine injections are recommended for nociceptive pain, whereas gabapentin, pregabalin, tricyclic antidepressants, and morphine are advised for neuropathic pain. Mexiletine, levetiracetam, and quinine sulfate help patients with cramps. Assistive devices such as special mattresses, pillows, and wheelchairs may help to prevent pain. Ultimately, many patients require nonopioid analgesics and anti-inflammatory medications, and when these fail, opioids become the mainstay of pain treatment. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Lafora Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Seizure Management
retrieved_docs.content: Perampanel is a selective, noncompetitive antagonist of a type of α-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazolepropionic acid glutamate receptor that is usually used for the treatment of refractory focal onset seizures but is also effective for generalized epilepsy. The effectiveness of perampanel in Lafora disease was demonstrated by 2 case studies where it was used as first-line monotherapy and an add-on medication. In one case, a 15-year-old female with Lafora disease was treated with 10 mg of perampanel as monotherapy. The treatment resulted in a significant and dramatic decrease in seizure frequency and improved neurological and cognitive functioning. The second case was a 21-year-old Turkish female given 8 to 10 mg of perampanel in addition to a regimen that included clonazepam, levetiracetam, piracetam, valproate, zonisamide, a ketogenic diet, and vagal nerve stimulation. This was followed by seizure remission for more than 3 months and was associated with decreased epileptiform discharges on EEG.

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonus -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment of Cortical Myoclonus
retrieved_docs.content: Cortical hyperexcitability is an intrinsic cause of cortical myoclonus, and, consistent with this, anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs) are the usual first-line treatment approach. That said, AEDs can, in some cases, worsen the myoclonus. [61] Valproic acid, levetiracetam, clonazepam, and perampanel are commonly used, with at minimum some case reports as the underlying evidence base (although not all agree fully) for each agent. retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phenylbutyrate is a histone deacetylase inhibitor that decreases the stress response in the endoplasmic reticulum. Combined with taurursodiol (which increases the threshold for cellular apoptosis), it decreases neuronal destruction. The result is a slower decline in function. Sodium phenylbutyrate-taurursodiol is administered intrathecally and may result in myelitis, radiculitis, aseptic meningitis, and elevated intracranial pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease-modifying Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Riluzole, an NMDA (N-methyl-D-aspartate) receptor antagonist, reduces glutaminergic transmission by acting on the presynaptic voltage-gated sodium channels. Relative glutamate excess has been shown to lead to upper and lower motor neuron excitotoxicity contributing to neuronal cell death. [54] It has been shown to be associated with a statistically significant improvement in tracheostomy free survival. [55] Though sustained use for 18 months was not associated with improved muscle strength, it has been shown to improve survival by three months (equitable to a 9 percent increase in one-year survival). [56] It was found to be very useful in those with moderate functional impairment in those with advanced disease when used on a compassionate basis. [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonus -- Treatment / Management -- Perampanel
retrieved_docs.content: A relative newcomer among AEDs, perampanel gained FDA approval in 2012. Though its exact mechanism is unknown, it is believed to antagonize α-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazolepropionic acid (AMPA), thus reducing glutamate-mediated neuronal excitation. As with other AEDs used in myoclonus, the evidence base for perampanel in myoclonus is thin, consisting primarily of small (eg, n of 2) [63] cases series suggesting its utility in cortical myoclonus arising in the myoclonic epilepsies [64] and in posthypoxic myoclonus. [65] [66] Behavioral adverse effects include a range of dysfunction: worsening depression, hostility, aggression, suicidal and homicidal ideation, and abuse. Common side effects include gastrointestinal discomfort, vertigo, headache, fatigue, and weight gain, among many others. [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: PXT3003 (a combination of baclofen, naltrexone, and d-sorbitol) has demonstrated efficacy in reducing the toxic effects of PMP22 overexpression in both mice and humans. Subjects treated with PXT3003 showed no deterioration or improvement in CMT Neuropathy score (CMTNS), Overall Neuropathy Limitations Scale (ONLS), the 10-m walk test, and conduction velocities compared to placebo. PXT3003 was well tolerated and safe. [93] Additionally, curcumin has shown promise in alleviating endoplasmic reticulum stress and improving MPZ -associated neuropathy in murine models. [94]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Motor Neuron Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Because there is no cure for ALS and the prognosis is grave, clinicians must diagnose the disease with confirmation by NCS and needle EMG as soon as possible so that the appropriate interventions can be taken. There are only a few approved FDA treatments for ALS. Riluzole blocks glutamate release from neurons and has been shown to prolong survival by a few months. Riluzole can cause an elevation in liver enzymes; therefore, liver function testing should be ordered before administering this medication, as well as periodically. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: One classic presentation of ALS may not exist. Personalized therapy needs to be implemented. [3] Each patient must be evaluated for ALS mimics. Treatment at multidisciplinary ALS clinics improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the etiology of ALS. Identify the typical findings on the examination of a patient with ALS. Determine the interventions available for patients with ALS. Apply effective strategies to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members to facilitate positive outcomes for patients with ALS. retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Motor Neuron Disease -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Motor nerve conduction studies should examine the median, ulnar, and peroneal muscles. Findings consistent with ALS include normal or decreased compound muscle action potential (CMAP), prolongation of distal motor latency, and slow conduction velocity. Typically, CMAP should be normal as the myelin is intact, but due to axonal loss, CMAPs and conduction velocity may be decreased. Distal motor latency should also be normal but can be increased with axonal loss. [14] MMNCB, as described earlier, can be a mimic of ALS. In this disease, there is also a decreased CMAP due to a conduction block. [11] Conduction blocks are evidence of demyelination and reflect myelin pathology rather than axonal pathology. Conduction blocks should not be seen on NCS in ALS patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2911
retrieved_docs.content: 4‑Aminopyridine is a pharmacologic agent that may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By blocking voltage‑gated potassium channels, 4‑aminopyridine can improve nerve conduction and enhance motor function in patients with multiple sclerosis, thereby serving as a therapeutic option to alleviate some of the disease’s neurological symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1364
retrieved_docs.content: Baclofen and dantrolene are both pharmacologic agents that may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis. These medications are considered for managing the muscle spasticity and related motor symptoms that commonly occur in patients with multiple sclerosis, offering therapeutic options to improve functional ability and quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2758
retrieved_docs.content: Baclofen, a GABA‑B receptor agonist commonly employed as a muscle relaxant, may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis. In patients with multiple sclerosis, baclofen can help alleviate spasticity and muscle stiffness, which are frequent neurological manifestations of the disease. By reducing excessive neuronal firing in the spinal cord, baclofen contributes to improved motor function and comfort, making it a valuable therapeutic option for managing the motor symptoms associated with multiple sclerosis.

In clinical practice, Parkinson’s disease is frequently associated with اضطرابات اكتئابية (depressive disorders) and رعاش (tremor), highlighting the importance of addressing both motor and non‑motor symptoms when selecting therapeutic strategies. The inclusion of multiple pharmacologic options reflects the heterogeneous nature of Parkinson’s disease and the need for individualized treatment plans that can alleviate tremor, improve motor function, and potentially mitigate associated depressive symptoms.

Drug‑drug contraindications are noted: desvenlafaxine succinate and duloxetine hydrochloride are contraindicated with the antibiotic linezolid, reflecting a class‑wide interaction between serotonergic antidepressants and linezolid; linezolid is also contraindicated with trazodone. Bupropion and its salt forms are contraindicated in patients experiencing SSRI‑withdrawal syndrome. Overall, the knowledge network depicts a hierarchical and intersecting pharmacologic landscape in which CNS‑active substances, psychotropic drugs, antidepressants, nootropics, centrally acting sympathomimetics, anti‑dementia agents, smoking‑cessation agents and related withdrawal‑treatment agents are linked through parent‑child, isa, inverse_isa, causative‑agent, therapeutic‑use and contraindication relationships, guiding clinicians in selection, monitoring and safety management of treatments for neurological, psychiatric and behavioral conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: The antiepileptic therapeutic class (EPC) comprises a broad spectrum of agents used to treat الصرع (epilepsy), including subtypes such as benign neonatal epilepsy, and aims to prevent severe neurological sequelae such as coma, stupor, and brain damage. Core EPC members include valproate (and its calcium, sodium, magnesium salts and related derivatives), lamotrigine, tiagabine (including tiagabine hydrochloride), ethotoin, phenytoin (phenytoin sodium, mephenytoin), ethosuximide, felbamate, fosphenytoin (fosphenytoin sodium), gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide, levetiracetam, methsuximide, oxcarbazepine, primidone, trimethadione, topiramate, vigabatrin, zonisamide, and others. These drugs exert anticonvulsant effects through distinct mechanisms, allowing clinicians to tailor seizure control to individual patients and specific seizure types. Polypharmacy considerations are highlighted by observed co‑occurrence patterns: dextropropoxyfen frequently co‑occurs with phenytoin, and phenytoin often co‑occurs with the opioid analgesic oxycodone, underscoring the need for vigilance regarding drug‑drug interactions. Oxygen deprivation (hypoxia) is closely associated with the same critical outcomes—coma, stupor, and irreversible brain injury—because insufficient cerebral oxygen supply can precipitate a spectrum of unconscious states and permanent neuronal damage. Recognizing the interrelationship between epilepsy, its antiepileptic treatments, hypoxic events, and the potential for severe neurological impairment is essential for individualized therapy, careful monitoring of concomitant medications, and timely intervention to prevent lasting neurological dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Dantrolen and baklofen are classified as centrally acting muscle relaxants, a group of agents that reduce skeletal muscle tone by acting on the central nervous system. These medications are used to manage conditions characterized by excessive muscle contraction, such as spasticity and certain movement disorders, and their shared mechanism of central action underlies their therapeutic role in alleviating muscle-related symptoms.

Parkinson’s disease is clinically associated with اضطرابات اكتئابية (depressive disorders) and رعاش (tremor). Recognizing both the range of potential therapeutic options and the common clinical features of depression and tremor is essential for comprehensive patient care.
